section.title: The Wall Completed Amid Plots
section.sectionRationale: With the breaches closed and only the gates left to hang, the enemy shifts from open threat to cunning. Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem try four times to lure Nehemiah down to a parley at Ono, then send an open letter of slander, then hire a prophet to frighten him into the temple. Nehemiah refuses every trap, keeps his hands to the work, and the wall is finished in fifty-two days, so plainly by the hand of God that the surrounding nations lose their nerve. It is one movement: the final assault by plot, and the completion that silences it.
section.thesis: Single-minded devotion to God's work, guarded by discernment and prayer, refuses every lure and fear the enemy invents, so that when the wall is finished the nations themselves confess it was done by the help of God.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the invitation to Ono and Nehemiah's great-work reply, verses 1 to 4; the open letter of slander and the prayer for strength, verses 5 to 9; the hired prophet exposed by discernment, verses 10 to 14; and the wall completed to the fear of the nations, verses 15 to 19.

units.label: The invitation to Ono
units.note: I am doing a great work and cannot come down

verses.text: When Sanballat, Tobiah, Geshem the Arab, and the rest of our enemies heard that I had rebuilt the wall and not a gap was left, though to that time I had not yet installed the doors in the gates,
Sanballat and Geshem sent me this message: “Come, let us meet together in one of the villages on the plain of Ono.” But they were planning to harm me.
So I sent messengers to them, saying, “I am doing a great {{melakah|work}} and cannot come down. Why should the work stop while I leave it to go down to you?”
Four times they sent me the same message, and each time I gave the same reply.

units.label: The open letter of slander
units.note: you are inventing them in your own mind

verses.text: The fifth time, Sanballat sent me this same message by his young servant, who had in his hand an unsealed letter

that read: “It is reported among the nations, and Geshem agrees, that you and the Jews are plotting to revolt, and this is why you are building the wall. According to these reports, you are to become their king,
and you have even appointed prophets in Jerusalem to proclaim on your behalf: ‘There is a king in Judah.’ Soon these rumors will reach the ears of the king. So come, let us confer together.”
Then I sent him this reply: “There is nothing to these rumors you are spreading; you are inventing them in your own mind.”
For they were all trying to {{yare|frighten}} us, saying, “Their hands will be weakened in the work, and it will never be finished.” But now, my God, {{chazaq|strengthen}} my hands.

units.label: The hired prophet exposed
units.note: should a man like me run away

verses.text: Later, I went to the house of Shemaiah son of Delaiah, the son of Mehetabel, who was confined to his house. He said: “Let us meet at the house of God inside the temple. Let us shut the temple doors because they are coming to kill you, by night they are coming to kill you!”
But I replied, “Should a man like me run away? Should one like me go into the temple to save his own life? I will not go!”
I realized that God had not sent him, but that he had uttered this prophecy against me because Tobiah and Sanballat had hired him.
He had been hired to intimidate me so that I would sin by doing as he suggested, so they could give me a bad name in order to discredit me.
O my God, remember Tobiah and Sanballat for what they have done, and also Noadiah the prophetess and the other prophets who tried to intimidate me.
So the wall was completed in fifty-two days, on the twenty-fifth of Elul.
When all our enemies heard about this, all the surrounding nations were afraid and disheartened, for they realized that this task had been {{asah|accomplished}} by our God.
Also in those days, the nobles of Judah sent many letters to Tobiah, and Tobiah’s letters kept coming to them.
For many in Judah were bound by oath to him, since he was a son-in-law of Shecaniah son of Arah, and his son Jehohanan had married the daughter of Meshullam son of Berechiah.
Moreover, these nobles kept reporting to me Tobiah’s good deeds, and they relayed my words to him. And Tobiah sent letters to intimidate me.

melakah.gloss: Work, task, occupation, business. Nehemiah names the rebuilding a melakah, a great work with a divine weight to it; the same word describes the labor of the tabernacle and the rest God took on the seventh day. Because the task is God's, he will not come down from it for a parley with enemies.

yare.gloss: To fear, to be afraid, to dread. The enemies' whole strategy is to make Israel's hands drop through yare, fear, so the work is abandoned. Nehemiah refuses to let dread, whether of armies, slander, or a hired prophet, dictate his obedience, and turns instead to God for strength.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, to strengthen, to make firm, to grip. Against the enemy's aim to weaken the builders' hands, Nehemiah prays the opposite, that God would chazaq, strengthen, his hands. It is the language of God putting resolve and firmness into a servant so the work is carried through.

asah.gloss: To do, to make, to accomplish. When the wall is finished the nations see that the task was asah, done, by Israel's God; the human building is real, yet the achievement is unmistakably God's own doing. The word confesses that the help behind the work was divine.

basic.context: The wall is almost done, only the gate doors are left to hang, and the enemy changes tactics from force to trickery. Sanballat and Geshem invite Nehemiah to meet on the plain of Ono, four times, meaning to harm him; he answers that he is doing a great work and will not come down. Then comes an open letter accusing him of plotting to make himself king, meant to scare him; he denies it and prays, my God, strengthen my hands. Then a prophet named Shemaiah, secretly hired, urges him to hide in the temple to save his life; Nehemiah discerns the plot, refuses to sin by fleeing, and keeps working. In fifty-two days the wall is finished, and even the surrounding nations realize the work was done by God. The point is clear: stay focused on God's work, refuse fear and false counsel, and let God's evident help be the answer to every plot.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative, showing us how a godly leader met opposition, not a set of rules to copy step for step. The abiding truths are single-minded devotion to God's calling, the discernment to test counsel even when it is dressed as prophecy, the refusal to let fear drive us into sin, and the confidence that God finishes what He begins. Nehemiah's prayers against his enemies belong to his office and moment; under Christ we pray for enemies, yet we may still ask God to remember and to vindicate.
basic.originalLanguages: Nehemiah calls the rebuilding a melakah, a great work with God's weight on it, so he will not come down from it. The enemy's whole aim is yare, fear, to make the builders' hands drop; against that Nehemiah prays that God would chazaq, strengthen, his hands. And when the wall is finished the nations see it was asah, done, by God Himself.
basic.typology: Nehemiah setting his face to finish the work and refusing every lure to abandon it is a shadow of Christ, who set His face toward Jerusalem and would not be turned from the work the Father gave Him. The completed wall, achieved against every plot, points to the God who completes what He begins, finally in the finished work of the cross and the new Jerusalem.
basic.christ: Nehemiah would not come down from the wall, and he would not run into the temple to save himself; he stayed at his God-given task whatever the cost. In this he faintly pictures Jesus, who set His face to go to Jerusalem, refused Satan's short-cuts in the wilderness, and would not come down from the cross to save Himself, because He was doing the greatest work, and cannot come down. Where Nehemiah finished a wall, Christ finished redemption and cried, It is finished.
basic.god: God is the one who strengthens His servants' hands and finishes His work. He answers Nehemiah's short, urgent prayers, gives discernment to expose the hired prophet, and completes the wall so plainly that pagan nations are unnerved. He does not merely help from a distance; the achievement is confessed as His own doing.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 9:51 shows Jesus setting His face toward Jerusalem, undeflected. Philippians 1:6 promises that God will complete the good work He began. And 1 John 4:1 tells us to test the spirits, as Nehemiah tested Shemaiah's word.
basic.oneStory: The rebuilt wall is one more step in God preserving and re-gathering His people around His city and His house, against every effort to stop them. Nehemiah's finished, God-helped work points forward to the Christ who finishes the true work of redemption and to the new Jerusalem whose walls no enemy can breach.

context.literary: This chapter is the climax of the opposition that has run through the book: from mockery, to threatened attack, to internal strain, and now to cunning plots aimed at the leader himself. It is structured as a sequence of escalating traps, invitation, slander, and false prophecy, each met by Nehemiah's refusal and prayer, and resolved by the terse, triumphant note that the wall was finished in fifty-two days. Nehemiah's first-person memoir voice, with its arrow prayers, gives the account its intimacy and urgency.
context.historical: Ono lay about a day's journey northwest of Jerusalem, in neutral territory, which is why the meeting there was a trap Nehemiah could not risk. Sanballat governed Samaria, Tobiah was an Ammonite official with marriage ties inside Judah, and Geshem led an Arab confederation, so Nehemiah was ringed by hostile powers on every side. An open, unsealed letter was a calculated insult, meant to spread its accusation publicly and pressure him. Hiding in the temple was forbidden to a layman, so fleeing there would have been both cowardice and sin, discrediting Nehemiah before the people. The wall's completion on the twenty-fifth of Elul, in fifty-two days, is a remarkable pace given the opposition.
context.canonical: Nehemiah stands among the returns from exile, alongside Ezra, as God re-establishes His people in the land, a stage on the way to the coming of Christ. The theme of finishing God's work against opposition echoes the temple rebuilding under Haggai and Zechariah and looks ahead to the Lord who completes the work of salvation. The secure, walled city anticipates the final city of God, the new Jerusalem, whose walls stand forever.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record, from Nehemiah's own hand, how relentless and varied the opposition to God's work became, and how single-minded devotion, discernment, and dependence on God carried the work through to completion, so that the finished wall testified to God's help before the watching nations.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes how Nehemiah met specific plots in his own office and era; it does not prescribe every detail, such as his imprecations against enemies, as the pattern for believers under Christ. What endures is the call to stay focused on God's work, to test counsel and refuse fear, and to trust the God who finishes what He starts.

debates.question: Was Nehemiah right to refuse Shemaiah's counsel to flee into the temple?

views.label: Right, because it was a hired trap and a sin
views.case: Nehemiah discerned that God had not sent Shemaiah and that Tobiah and Sanballat had hired him; fleeing into the temple would have been an act of unbelieving fear, and for a layman a transgression, giving the enemy grounds to discredit him. His refusal was faith and integrity, not stubbornness.

views.label: A hard call requiring genuine spiritual discernment
views.case: The advice came clothed as prophecy and played on a real threat, so it could have seemed like prudent self-preservation; Nehemiah had to weigh it against God's word and character rather than his own safety, showing that even godly-sounding counsel must be tested.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage teaches that fear is a poor counselor and that all counsel, however spiritual it sounds, must be tested against God's word and God's ways.

debates.question: How should we read Nehemiah's prayer, remember Tobiah and Sanballat for what they have done?

views.label: A committal of judgment to God
views.case: Rather than taking revenge, Nehemiah hands his enemies over to God, asking the righteous Judge to deal with their treachery; it is an act of trust that leaves vengeance in God's hands, not his own.

views.label: An Old Covenant imprecation to be read through Christ
views.case: Such prayers against enemies belong to the era before Christ's clearer command to love and pray for enemies; we read them as honest cries for justice that are ultimately answered and reframed at the cross, where mercy and judgment meet.

debates.bearing: Both readings restrain personal vengeance and rest justice with God; under Christ we add intercession for our enemies, while still longing for God to set all wrongs right.

study.originalLanguages: Nehemiah calls the rebuilding a melakah, a work or task, but plainly a great one with God's own weight upon it; it is the word used of the labor of the tabernacle and of the work God rested from on the seventh day. Because this is God's melakah, Nehemiah will not come down from it for the enemy's parley. The whole strategy against him is summed up in one root, yare, to fear: the letters and the hired prophet are all designed to make Israel's hands drop through dread so the wall is abandoned. Against that assault Nehemiah prays its exact opposite, that God would chazaq, strengthen, make firm, grip his hands, the language of God putting resolve into a servant so the task is carried through. And when the wall stands finished, the nations themselves confess what the vocabulary has been building toward: the work was asah, done, accomplished, by Israel's God. Work, fear, strength, and accomplishment: a servant clinging to God's task, refusing dread, strengthened by God, and vindicated when God finishes what He began.

typology.type: Nehemiah refusing to come down from the great work
typology.fulfillment: Christ setting His face toward Jerusalem and refusing to come down from the cross, finishing the work the Father gave Him, Luke 9:51 and John 19:30.

typology.type: The hired false prophet exposed by discernment
typology.fulfillment: Christ and His people testing the spirits and unmasking false prophecy, resisting every temptation dressed as spiritual counsel, Matthew 4:1-11 and 1 John 4:1.

typology.type: The wall completed against all opposition
typology.fulfillment: God completing the work He begins, in the finished work of the cross and the secure walls of the new Jerusalem, Philippians 1:6 and Revelation 21:12.

study.christ: Nehemiah's great line, I am doing a great work and cannot come down, reaches its fullness in Jesus. Twice tempted to abandon His task, in the wilderness by Satan's short-cuts and at the cross by the mockers' taunt to come down and save Himself, Christ would not be moved, because He was doing the greatest work, and would not come down from it. Nehemiah would not flee into the temple to save his own life; Jesus refused to save His own life so that He might save ours. Where Nehemiah discerned and resisted a hired prophet, Christ perfectly discerned and resisted the tempter. And where Nehemiah finished a wall in fifty-two days, Jesus finished redemption and declared, It is finished, securing a city whose walls will never fall.
study.god: God is the strengthener and finisher of His work. He hears Nehemiah's brief, urgent prayers and puts firmness into his hands; He grants the discernment that exposes the hired prophet before the trap can spring; and He brings the wall to completion at a pace that leaves hostile nations afraid and disheartened. The text is careful to give Him the credit: the task had been accomplished by our God. He is not a distant helper but the true builder, who guards His people, completes His purposes, and makes even His enemies acknowledge His hand.

crossReferences.note: Jesus set His face to go to Jerusalem, undeflected from His work, as Nehemiah would not come down from his.
He who began a good work will carry it on to completion, the God who finishes the wall and finishes salvation.
Test the spirits, whether they are from God, as Nehemiah tested and exposed the hired prophecy of Shemaiah.
An earlier plot frustrated because God had thwarted it, the same pattern of opposition met by God's protecting help.

study.oneStory: The finished wall is one more advance in God's long work of preserving and re-gathering His people around His city and His house after the exile, on the road that leads to Christ. Every plot to stop the work fails, and the achievement is confessed as God's own doing. Nehemiah's single-minded, God-strengthened, God-completed labor points forward to Jesus, who would not come down until the true work was finished, and to the new Jerusalem whose walls no enemy will ever breach.

quiz.q: How did Nehemiah answer the repeated invitation to meet on the plain of Ono?

options.text: He refused, saying he was doing a great work and could not come down.
options.note: Verses 3-4 record his fourfold refusal to leave the work for a parley meant to harm him.

options.text: He agreed to go once he had finished the gates.
options.note: He did not agree at all; he saw the invitation as a trap and stayed at the work.

options.text: He sent Ezra to meet them in his place.
options.note: No delegate is mentioned; Nehemiah simply refused to come down.

options.text: He ignored the message entirely and said nothing.
options.note: He answered clearly and repeatedly, four times, with the same reply.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of the open letter accusing Nehemiah of plotting to become king?

options.text: To frighten and discredit him so the work would be abandoned.
options.note: Verse 9 shows they were all trying to frighten and weaken the builders' hands.

options.text: To warn him sincerely about a real Persian investigation.
options.note: The letter used the threat of the king's ears, but its aim was intimidation, not honest warning.

options.text: To offer him a genuine political alliance.
options.note: It was slander, not an alliance; Nehemiah called the reports invented.

options.text: To congratulate him on the rebuilt wall.
options.note: The letter was hostile slander, not congratulation.

quiz.q: Why did Nehemiah refuse Shemaiah's advice to hide in the temple?

options.text: He discerned Shemaiah was hired and that fleeing in fear would be sin.
options.note: Verses 11-13 show he saw God had not sent him and refused to sin and be discredited.

options.text: He was not afraid of the threat at all and stayed only out of pride.
options.note: The threat was real, but his refusal was faith and integrity, not mere pride.

options.text: He believed the temple offered no real safety anyway.
options.note: The issue was not the temple's safety but that fleeing there would be unbelief and, for a layman, a sin.

options.text: He wanted to consult the prophets first.
options.note: He discerned Shemaiah himself was a hired false prophet; he did not seek more such counsel.

quiz.q: How long did the rebuilding of the wall take, and how did the nations respond?

options.text: Fifty-two days, and the surrounding nations were afraid, seeing it was done by God.
options.note: Verses 15-16 give the fifty-two days and the nations' fear at God's evident work.

options.text: Fifty-two days, and the nations celebrated with Nehemiah.
options.note: The timing is right, but the nations were disheartened and afraid, not celebrating.

options.text: Fifty-two years, showing the slow work of faith.
options.note: It was fifty-two days, not years; the speed is part of the point.

options.text: A few days, with no reaction from anyone.
options.note: It took fifty-two days, and the nations reacted with fear at God's hand.

quiz.q: How does Nehemiah's great work point us to Christ?

options.text: As Nehemiah would not come down from his work, Christ would not come down from the cross until redemption was finished.
options.note: Nehemiah's single-minded task foreshadows Christ who set His face to His work and finished it.

options.text: It shows that God's work is always easy once you refuse distractions.
options.note: The chapter shows the work was hard and opposed; the link to Christ is His refusal to abandon His task, not ease.

options.text: It teaches that leaders should never listen to any advice.
options.note: Nehemiah tested counsel rather than rejecting all counsel; the point is discernment, not deafness.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ; it is only ancient history.
options.note: Nehemiah's finished, God-helped work clearly foreshadows Christ's finished work.

questions: Nehemiah answered every distraction with, I am doing a great work and cannot come down. What is the God-given work in your life that you are most tempted to come down from, and what would it look like to stay?

The enemy's whole strategy was to make God's people afraid so their hands would drop. Where is fear, of failure, of what people say, of loss, quietly weakening your hands in the work God has given you?
Shemaiah's dangerous advice came dressed as prophecy. How do you test counsel that sounds spiritual, and where might you need more discernment about voices urging you to act out of fear?
Nehemiah met each threat with a short, honest prayer, my God, strengthen my hands. How could you build that kind of immediate, dependent prayer into the pressured moments of your day?
When the wall was finished, even outsiders saw it was done by God. How does longing for God to be seen in your work change the way you labor and the way you handle opposition?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnotes offer in Kephirim for the villages and in the valley for the plain of Ono; the site lay in neutral ground about a day from Jerusalem, which is why the proposed meeting was so risky.
Geshem here renders the Hebrew Gashmu, a variant of the same name in verse 1; the unsealed, open letter was meant to broadcast its accusation publicly.
The Hebrew reads simply But now; the BSB adds my God for clarity, so the line becomes the arrow prayer, But now, my God, strengthen my hands.
Elul is the sixth month of the Hebrew calendar, falling in late summer, around August and September; the wall was finished on its twenty-fifth day, in fifty-two days of building.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Nehemiah 6:1-19. Em dashes in verses 1, 6, and 10 were converted to commas or clean breaks; no em or en dashes remain. Heading and poem markers and footnote callers were ignored per instructions.
